title: angelica archangelica
intro: Angelica archangelica, also known as wild celery, is a perennial plant native to Europe and Asia, belonging to the carrot family (Apiaceae). This plant has been used in traditional medicine for centuries, valued for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antiviral properties. The health benefits of angelica archangelica include reducing fever, relieving digestive issues, and alleviating respiratory problems, making it a popular remedy for colds, flu, and other illnesses. In herbal preparations, angelica root and leaves are often dried or made into teas, tinctures, and infusions to harness its medicinal properties and promote overall well-being.
benefits_desc: Angelica archangelica is used to promote overall health and well-being, with its numerous benefits including reducing inflammation and relieving pain, as well as aiding in digestion and reducing symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome. Its medicinal uses also extend to treating respiratory issues such as bronchitis and coughs, as well as reducing fever and alleviating menstrual cramps. Additionally, angelica archangelica has been found to have antioxidant properties, which can help protect against cell damage and reduce the risk of chronic diseases like cancer and heart disease. By incorporating angelica archangelica into one's diet or using it as a natural remedy, individuals can potentially experience a range of health benefits, from improved immune function to enhanced cognitive function.

benefits_list.name: antimicrobial properties
benefits_list.desc: angelica archangelica has been shown to exhibit antimicrobial properties, making it effective against a range of bacteria, viruses, and fungi.

benefits_list.name: digestive aid
benefits_list.desc: the plant's roots and rhizomes have been used to treat digestive issues such as bloating, gas, and indigestion due to its ability to stimulate digestion and relieve symptoms.

benefits_list.name: anti-inflammatory properties
benefits_list.desc: angelica archangelica contains compounds with anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce swelling and pain in the body.

benefits_list.name: antioxidant properties
benefits_list.desc: the plant has antioxidant properties, which help protect the body from oxidative stress and cell damage caused by free radicals.

benefits_list.name: immune system support
benefits_list.desc: angelica archangelica has been traditionally used to support the immune system and help the body fight off infections and diseases.

benefits_list.name: menstrual relief
benefits_list.desc: the plant has been used to treat menstrual cramps, bloating, and other symptoms associated with pms due to its ability to relax the uterine muscles and reduce inflammation.

benefits_list.name: skin and wound healing
benefits_list.desc: angelica archangelica has been used topically to treat skin irritations, wounds, and burns due to its antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties.

actions_desc: Angelica archangelica has demonstrated therapeutic actions that include anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and expectorant properties, affecting the respiratory system by reducing inflammation and easing congestion. Additionally, its nervine and sedative actions influence the nervous system, promoting relaxation and reducing anxiety. The plant's diuretic and emetic properties also have an impact on the urinary system, while its anti-septic and vulnerary actions affect the integumentary system, aiding in wound healing. Furthermore, its antipyretic and diaphoretic properties influence the thermoregulatory system, helping to reduce fever and promote sweating.

actions_list.desc: prevents the growth of microorganisms and promotes wound healing.

actions_list.desc: relaxes muscles and relieves cramps, spasms, and tremors.

actions_list.desc: supports liver function and promotes the removal of toxins from the body.

actions_list.desc: inhibits the growth of bacteria and helps to prevent infections.

constituents_desc: Angelica archangelica has been found to contain a diverse array of bioactive constituents, including sesquiterpenes, flavonoids, coumarins, lignans, phenolic acids, and volatile oils. The sesquiterpenes present in this plant, such as sesquiterpene lactones, exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, while the flavonoids, including kaempferol and quercetin, demonstrate anti-inflammatory and cardiovascular protective effects. The coumarins, including scopoletin and umbelliferone, display antioxidant and antimicrobial properties, and the lignans, including sesamin and sesamolin, have been shown to possess antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities. Additionally, the volatile oils and phenolic acids present in Angelica archangelica, such as bergamotene and ferulic acid, contribute to its overall bioactivity and potential health benefits.

parts_desc: The most commonly used parts of Angelica archangelica are the roots, leaves, and seeds. The roots are employed in various herbal remedies to treat respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and coughs, as well as digestive problems like bloating and indigestion, while also being used to relieve menstrual cramps and arthritis. The leaves and stems are used to make a tea that helps to alleviate fever, colds, and flu, and can also be used topically to treat skin irritations and wounds. The seeds of the Angelica plant are used as a diuretic to treat kidney problems and bladder issues, as well as being employed in traditional medicine to relieve anxiety and insomnia.

parts_list.name: leaves
parts_list.desc: the leaves of the angelica archangelica plant are large, alternate, and compound, consisting of 3-5 leaflets with serrated edges, and have a distinctive aroma.

parts_list.name: root
parts_list.desc: the root of angelica archangelica is a thick, fleshy taproot that is used in traditional medicine for its medicinal properties.

parts_list.name: stem
parts_list.desc: the stem of the angelica archangelica plant is thick, green, and hollow, with a diameter of up to 2 cm, and is used in traditional medicine for its medicinal properties.

parts_list.name: flowers
parts_list.desc: the flowers of the angelica archangelica plant are small, yellowish-green, and arranged in large compound umbels, which are highly fragrant and used in traditional medicine for their medicinal properties.

parts_list.name: seeds
parts_list.desc: the seeds of the angelica archangelica plant are small, yellowish-brown, and contained within the fruit, which is a berry-like structure, and are used in traditional medicine for their medicinal properties.

parts_list.name: resin
parts_list.desc: the resin of the angelica archangelica plant is a sticky, fragrant substance that is secreted by the plant and is used in traditional medicine for its medicinal properties.

parts_list.name: rhizome
parts_list.desc: the rhizome of the angelica archangelica plant is a thick, underground stem that produces new plants and is used in traditional medicine for its medicinal properties.

preparations_desc: Angelica archangelica most common herbal preparations are infusions, tinctures, and glycerites. Infusions, also known as teas, are often used to support digestive health, reduce inflammation, and alleviate cold and flu symptoms, making them a popular remedy for issues such as indigestion, bloating, and fever. Tinctures, typically made by steeping the roots and stems of the angelica plant in a solvent like ethanol, are commonly employed to treat respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and asthma, as well as to alleviate menstrual cramps and menopausal symptoms. Glycerites, which are made by infusing the angelica plant in a glycerin base, are often used to support liver and kidney function, while also providing relief from anxiety, insomnia, and stress.

preparations_list.name: angelica root infusion
preparations_list.desc: a soothing tea made by steeping dried angelica root in hot water, often used to calm digestive issues and promote relaxation.

preparations_list.name: angelica tincture
preparations_list.desc: a concentrated liquid extract made by steeping angelica root in a solvent such as ethanol, often used to support immune function and reduce inflammation.

preparations_list.name: angelica oil
preparations_list.desc: an essential oil extracted from the leaves and roots of the angelica plant, often used in aromatherapy to promote relaxation and reduce stress.

preparations_list.name: angelica infused vinegar
preparations_list.desc: a liquid extract made by steeping angelica root in vinegar, often used to support digestion and reduce inflammation in the gut.

preparations_list.name: angelica salve
preparations_list.desc: a topical cream or ointment made by infusing angelica oil into a carrier oil, often used to soothe skin irritations and promote wound healing.

preparations_list.name: angelica glycerite
preparations_list.desc: a sweet, syrupy liquid extract made by infusing angelica root in glycerin, often used as a cough syrup or to soothe sore throats.

preparations_list.name: angelica extract capsules
preparations_list.desc: a dietary supplement made by encapsulating dried angelica root extract, often used to support immune function and reduce inflammation.

side_effects_desc: Angelica archangelica side effects are primarily associated with individuals who consume large quantities of the plant or its extracts. Common side effects include excessive sweating, dizziness, and stomach upset, which may lead to nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. People with pre-existing kidney or liver conditions, as well as pregnant or breastfeeding women, are more likely to experience side effects such as headaches, fatigue, and allergic reactions like skin rashes and itching. Precautions include avoiding the plant's use in excessive amounts, as it may cause interactions with medications, particularly blood thinners, and exacerbate conditions like high blood pressure and bleeding disorders.

side_effects_list.name: allergic reactions
side_effects_list.desc: some individuals may experience allergic reactions, including skin irritation, hives, or difficulty breathing, after consuming or coming into contact with angelica archangelica.

side_effects_list.name: digestive issues
side_effects_list.desc: consuming large amounts of angelica archangelica may cause stomach upset, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea in some individuals.

side_effects_list.name: interactions with medications
side_effects_list.desc: angelica archangelica may interact with certain medications, such as blood thinners, diabetes medications, and blood pressure medications, which can lead to adverse effects.

side_effects_list.name: photosensitivity
side_effects_list.desc: angelica archangelica may cause photosensitivity in some individuals, leading to skin irritation or discoloration after exposure to sunlight.

side_effects_list.name: respiratory problems
side_effects_list.desc: inhaling the volatile oils of angelica archangelica may trigger respiratory issues, such as asthma or bronchitis, in susceptible individuals.

side_effects_list.name: seizures
side_effects_list.desc: consuming large amounts of angelica archangelica may cause seizures in rare cases, particularly in individuals with a history of seizure disorders.

side_effects_list.name: uterine stimulation
side_effects_list.desc: angelica archangelica may stimulate the uterus, which can be problematic for pregnant women or those with a history of uterine problems.
